description: The provided image is unreadable and displays only a generic Check for updates UI icon rather than a scientific figure. Based on the accompanying paper context for Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel manufactured by selective laser melting (SLM), the intended figure likely presents data related to microstructural evolution or mechanical performance across SLM samples fabricated at laser powers of 70 W, 90 W, 110 W, 130 W, and 150 W, alongside an as-cast benchmark. The context reports that all SLM samples consist of single-phase austenite γ-Fe(M) with uniformly distributed nano-sized κ-carbide precipitates, exhibiting grain growth from 8.1 μm to 14.3 μm as laser energy density increases from 58.33 to 125.00 J/mm³. The 110 W sample reportedly achieves the best comprehensive mechanical properties, with a yield strength of 887.1 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 1076.8 MPa, and elongation of 40.80%. Nevertheless, specific visual elements including curve trajectories, microstructural morphologies, diffraction peaks, or scale bars remain unverifiable from the provided image.

Figure 1 presents a schematic illustration of the selective laser melting (SLM) setup used to fabricate Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel from spherical Fe30Mn9AlC3Ni0.6Cr powder. The main panel depicts the powder-bed configuration with a laser beam scanning over the build platform, a powder feed container, and an overflow container, indicating the moving direction of the feed mechanism. The lower-left inset details the scanning strategy, showing a 67° rotation between adjacent layers (Layer n and Layer n+1) to minimize laser vector coincidence and crystallographic texture. The lower-right inset provides a cross-sectional view of the melt pool, illustrating the interaction between the laser beam and the powder bed atop the fabricated part and substrate. The figure establishes the experimental framework for samples printed at laser powers of 70–150 W, corresponding to energy densities of 58.33–125.00 J/mm³, with a scanning speed of 750 mm/s, layer thickness of 0.02 mm, and hatch spacing of 0.08 mm.

Figure 2 presents comprehensive characterization of PREP-prepared Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni powder for selective laser melting. Subfigures (a) and (b) are SEM images showing a dense population of highly spherical particles with smooth surfaces and no visible satellites or irregular shapes; the scale bars indicate 400 μm in (a) and 10 μm in (b), with the latter revealing fine surface texture. Subfigures (c)–(f) are EDS elemental maps acquired from the same powder particle, demonstrating uniform distributions of Fe (yellow), Mn (blue), Al (red), and Ni (purple) throughout the sphere. Subfigure (g) is a cross-sectional SEM image with a 2 μm scale bar, revealing a fully solid internal structure with visible solidification substructures rather than hollow porosity. The text reports that the powder has a median particle size (D50) of 35.69 μm, a size range mainly of 15–53 μm, and excellent flowability with a Hall value of 14 s/50 g. Collectively, these images confirm the powder’s high sphericity, chemical homogeneity, and solid nature, supporting its suitability for SLM processing.

This figure presents the particle-size distribution of the PREP-prepared Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni powder used for selective laser melting. The chart displays a histogram (blue bars, left y-axis: Volume %) representing the frequency distribution of particle sizes, overlaid with a cumulative distribution curve (red line, right y-axis: Cumulative Volume %) showing the integrated volume fraction. The x-axis is labeled "Particle sizes (mm)" on a logarithmic scale spanning 0.1 to 1000, though the caption and text indicate the powder lies in the micrometer regime. According to the caption and citation, the particle sizes are mainly distributed in the 15–53 μm range, with a median particle size (D50) of 35.69 μm. The distribution appears unimodal and relatively narrow, peaking between approximately 20 and 60 μm on the plotted scale. This narrow size distribution, combined with the reported Hall flowability of 14 s/50 g, confirms the powder's excellent flowability and suitability for the SLM process, supporting the subsequent fabrication of low-density steel with controlled microstructure and mechanical properties.

Figure 4 displays the X-ray diffraction pattern of the PREP-prepared Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel powder, recorded over a 2θ range from 30° to 100°. The diffractogram reveals the coexistence of two phases: austenite γ-Fe(M), indicated by black circles, and ferrite α-Fe(M), indicated by red downward triangles. The γ-Fe(M) phase dominates the pattern with intense diffraction peaks indexed as (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222), whereas the α-Fe(M) phase is present only in minor amounts, showing weak (110) and (200) reflections. The caption and accompanying citation state that the relatively slow cooling during PREP preparation allows a small amount of carbon to dissolve in the α-Fe phase, thereby promoting minor ferrite formation. This dual-phase constitution in the feedstock powder provides a baseline that contrasts with the subsequent SLM-processed samples, in which rapid solidification reportedly suppresses ferrite and yields a fully austenitic microstructure. The figure thus establishes the starting phase composition of the precursor powder prior to selective laser melting.

Figure 5 presents the X-ray diffraction patterns of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel samples fabricated by selective laser melting (SLM) at five different laser powers: P-70, P-90, P-110, P-130, and P-150. All five curves display diffraction peaks indexed exclusively to the austenite phase γ-Fe(M) with visible reflections at (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222), and the legend confirms the phase identification. Notably, no α-Fe(M) phase is detected in any of the SLM-printed samples, unlike the precursor powder. The caption and accompanying text report that the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the (111) peak systematically decreases from 0.289 for P-70 to 0.237 for P-150, indicating a gradual increase in average grain size as the laser energy density rises. This figure supports the core conclusion that SLM processing with varying laser power produces a fully austenitic microstructure while enabling grain size control through energy density adjustment.

Figure 6 presents optical microscopy (OM) images of transverse cross-sections of selective laser melting (SLM)-printed Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel fabricated at different laser powers. Subfigures (a)–(e) correspond to samples P-70, P-90, P-110, P-130, and P-150, produced at laser powers of 70 W, 90 W, 110 W, 130 W, and 150 W, respectively. Image (a) shows severe porosity (4.75%) with numerous irregular dark pores and lack-of-fusion defects. Images (b) and (c) demonstrate significantly reduced porosity, with (c) P-110 achieving the minimum porosity of 0.13%. Images (d) and (e) reveal a subsequent increase in porosity to 0.28% and 0.35%, respectively, with (e) additionally exhibiting visible cracks as annotated. The caption states that porosity first decreases and then increases with increasing laser power, indicating an optimal processing window around P-110 for maximizing relative density while avoiding crack formation.

Figure 7 presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) inverse pole figure (IPF) maps of selective laser melted (SLM) Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel, alongside an austenite-phase IPF color key. Subfigures (a)–(e) display transverse cross-sections (x–y plane) of samples printed at laser powers of 70 W (P-70), 90 W (P-90), 110 W (P-110), 130 W (P-130), and 150 W (P-150), respectively. All five transverse maps reveal fine equiaxed austenite grains with seemingly random crystallographic orientations, attributed to the 67° layer rotation strategy employed during SLM; the average grain size visibly increases with rising laser power from 8.1 μm to 14.3 μm. Subfigure (f) shows the longitudinal section (x–z plane) of P-110, where columnar grains dominate and align along the deposition direction, with minor equiaxed grains at melt-pool bottoms. This contrast demonstrates that while in-plane thermal cycling and scanning rotation randomize grain orientations in the transverse view, the steep vertical thermal gradient during solidification drives epitaxial columnar growth across deposition layers, fundamentally linking SLM thermal history to microstructural anisotropy.

Figure 8 presents grain-size distribution histograms for selective laser melted (SLM) Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel samples fabricated under varying laser powers. Subfigures (a) through (e) display the transverse cross-sectional distributions for samples P-70, P-90, P-110, P-130, and P-150, with average grain sizes of 8.1, 10.6, 11.0, 13.9, and 14.3 μm, respectively. Subfigure (f) shows the longitudinal grain-size distribution for P-110, yielding a larger average of 14.9 μm compared to its transverse value. The histograms reveal a systematic right-shift and broadening of the distribution as laser power increases from 70 W to 150 W, indicating progressive grain coarsening. The pronounced difference between longitudinal and transverse sections in P-110 is attributed to anisotropic cooling: faster horizontal heat dissipation into the metallic substrate produces finer equiaxed grains, whereas slower vertical cooling against the gaseous atmosphere results in coarser columnar grains. These microstructural observations support the conclusion that laser power strongly governs grain size in SLM-processed austenitic low-density steels, thereby influencing the balance between porosity and grain-scale strengthening.

This figure presents a comprehensive TEM-based microstructural analysis of the P-110 Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel fabricated by selective laser melting. Panel (a) is a bright-field TEM image revealing dislocation tangles inside the austenite matrix. Panel (b) shows selected-area diffraction patterns (SADPs) along the [011] zone axis for both austenite γ and κ carbide, establishing their crystallographic orientation relationship. Panel (c) is a dark-field image using the (010)κ reflection, revealing that κ carbide consists of uniformly distributed nanoscale particles. Panel (d) provides a lower-magnification TEM view of a region with high-density dislocations. Panel (e) is a high-resolution TEM image of the austenite phase, resolving the (200) and (1-11) lattice planes with measured interplanar spacings of 0.262 nm and 0.223 nm, respectively. Panel (f) shows the corresponding fast Fourier transform confirming the [011] austenite zone axis. The caption states that ferrite diffraction patterns were not detected because rapid cooling during SLM suppressed ferrite formation, yielding an austenitic matrix containing nanoscale κ carbide precipitates and a high dislocation density.

Figure 10 displays the 2D and 3D surface topographies and arithmetic mean roughness (Sa) of selective laser melted (SLM) Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel fabricated at increasing laser powers. Subfigures (a)–(e) correspond to samples P-70, P-90, P-110, P-130, and P-150 (laser powers of 70 W, 90 W, 110 W, 130 W, and 150 W, respectively), with measured Sa values of 20.71 μm, 9.19 μm, 5.29 μm, 4.65 μm, and 4.19 μm. The color-coded height maps and rendered 3D views reveal that the as-built surface evolves from severely irregular, pronounced protrusions and deep valleys at low power to a markedly smoother morphology at higher power. This monotonic decrease in surface roughness with increasing laser power indicates enhanced melting stability and reduced lack-of-fusion or balling defects, supporting the conclusion that optimizing laser power is critical for improving the dimensional accuracy and surface quality of SLM-printed Fe–Mn–Al–C–Ni components.

This figure presents a series of SEM micrographs showing the top surface morphology of selective laser melted (SLM) Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel fabricated at laser powers of (a) 70 W, (b) 90 W, (c) 110 W, (d) 130 W, and (e) 150 W, with a scale bar of 200 μm. At 70 W (a), the surface exhibits severe lack of fusion, numerous unfused powder particles, and holes, indicating insufficient energy input. At 90 W (b), lack of fusion persists but is significantly reduced, reflecting improved inter-track fusion. At 110 W (c), the surface becomes notably smooth and dense with only sporadic balling defects, representing the optimal energy density window. As the power further increases to 130 W (d) and 150 W (e), the surfaces remain relatively flat but progressively develop balling phenomena—small spheroidized splashes indicated by yellow arrows—attributed to localized heat accumulation and excessive melt pool fluidity. Collectively, the sequence demonstrates a transition from lack-of-fusion-dominated to balling-dominated defects as laser power increases, supporting the conclusion that moderate power (110 W) yields the best surface quality for this alloy system.

Figure 12 presents the room-temperature engineering tensile stress–strain curves for Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel fabricated by selective laser melting (SLM) at laser powers of 70, 90, 110, 130, and 150 W (labeled P-70, P-90, P-110, P-130, and P-150), alongside an as-cast counterpart. The plot reveals that all SLM-processed samples exhibit significantly higher ultimate tensile strength and generally superior ductility compared to the as-cast alloy. Among the SLM variants, the P-110 sample achieves the optimal strength–ductility synergy, with a yield strength near 887 MPa, ultimate tensile strength around 1077 MPa, and elongation exceeding 40%. The P-70 curve terminates prematurely at approximately 20% strain, reflecting pronounced porosity and lack-of-fusion defects, whereas P-150 shows reduced plasticity (~28% elongation) associated with cracking and balling. The P-90 and P-130 samples display comparable high elongations (~39–40%) with tensile strengths above 1030 MPa. Collectively, the curves demonstrate that moderate laser power effectively suppresses deleterious defects while promoting favorable microstructural features, yielding mechanical properties that markedly surpass those of the conventionally cast material.

Figure 14 is a grouped bar chart illustrating the average Vickers hardness of the cross-sectional (Cross) and longitudinal (Longitudinal) sections of Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel fabricated by selective laser melting (SLM) at laser powers of 70 W (P-70), 90 W (P-90), 110 W (P-110), 130 W (P-130), and 150 W (P-150). The vertical axis indicates hardness in HV. For both section orientations, hardness increases with laser power from P-70 to P-110, reaching peak values of 294 HV (longitudinal) and 292 HV (cross-section), and then decreases at P-130 and P-150. The specific recorded values are 279 and 278 HV for P-70, 289 and 285 HV for P-90, 294 and 292 HV for P-110, 284 and 283 HV for P-130, and 283 and 281 HV for P-150, respectively, for longitudinal and cross sections. Error bars represent data variability. The figure indicates that longitudinal sections are consistently slightly harder than cross-sections, and the accompanying text attributes the hardness trend to the combined effects of grain size and internal defects such as pores, cracks, and unfused powder particles.

Figure 13 presents SEM fractographs of tensile fracture surfaces from selective laser melted Fe–30Mn–9Al–C–3Ni low-density steel processed at laser powers of 70–150 W (samples P-70 to P-150). Subfigure (a) for P-70 reveals unfused powder particles labeled as lack of fusion and internal cracks, correlating with high porosity and poor ductility (23.10% elongation). Subfigure (b) for P-90 still shows minor lack-of-fusion defects but increasingly dominant ductile dimple morphology. Subfigure (c) for P-110 exhibits fully ductile dimple fracture with a 2 μm-scale inset highlighting fine equiaxed dimples, consistent with the best strength–ductility combination (yield strength 887.1 MPa, elongation 40.80%). Subfigure (d) for P-130 displays a mixed fracture mode comprising dimples and cleavage facets, while subfigure (e) for P-150 shows pronounced cleavage planes, cracks, and reduced dimple density with a 2 μm inset confirming cleavage steps, matching decreased plasticity (28.28% elongation). The figure demonstrates that insufficient energy at low power causes lack-of-fusion porosity, whereas excessive power induces stress cracking and spheroidization, establishing an intermediate power of 110 W as optimal for ductile fracture and mechanical performance.
